structured_choices.data_source: The dataset will be sourced from publicly available repositories like arXiv and PubMed, comprising tens of thousands of articles across various academic domains from the past decade.
structured_choices.label_definition: The labels will be the abstracts accompanying the full texts in the dataset, providing a benchmark for evaluating the generative model's output.
structured_choices.split_method: A stratified random split will be implemented to ensure diversity in training, validation, and test sets, preserving proportional representation of academic domains.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: Feature preprocessing steps such as tokenization and text normalization will be fit on the training data and applied consistently across validation and test sets.
structured_choices.features: Primary features include the full text of papers and metadata such as domain and publication year. Text statistical features like word frequency and syntactic structure are also utilized.
structured_choices.model: The proposed model is a hierarchical attention mechanism designed to capture both document-level and section-level context.
structured_choices.baseline: The baseline is a traditional extractive summarization method, adjusted with equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort for fair comparison.
structured_choices.primary_metric: The primary evaluation metric will be a human evaluation score, focusing on clarity and informativeness, as these directly impact engagement and citation rates.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and model selection will occur on the validation set using ROUGE scores as the guiding metric.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set will be utilized only once for final evaluation after model selection, guaranteeing unbiased performance assessment.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Evaluator variability is addressed by ensuring a diverse panel with balanced expertise in various academic domains and providing standardized assessment criteria.

design_narrative: To test the hypothesis that a hierarchical attention mechanism can improve the relevance of generated abstracts, the experiment will leverage a dataset sourced from arXiv and PubMed. These repositories offer extensive coverage of academic texts across multiple domains, ensuring that the training data's diversity mirrors a real-world application. Labels are defined as existing abstracts, providing natural benchmarks for generative outputs. We will implement a stratified random split approach to construct training, validation, and test sets. This will preserve the proportional representation of academic domains within the datasets, thereby ensuring comprehensive coverage while safeguarding against potential biases. Preprocessing involves feature extraction, such as tokenization, normalization, and syntactic structuring, fitted against training data and applied likewise to other sets. The model to trial is a hierarchical attention mechanism, versatile enough to dissect document-level and section-level contexts, contrasted against a traditional extractive summarization baseline. Performance will be evaluated chiefly through human scoring on clarity and informativeness, pivotal for reader engagement and citation potential. Supplementary metrics like BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR provide quantitative checks against subjective evaluation variability. Model selection involves ROUGE-based tuning via validation data; utilizing the test set is reserved for ultimate assessment post-optimization. Human evaluators, possessing diverse backgrounds and disciplined grounding, will ensure reliability in subjective scoring by adhering to standardized evaluation parameters. Variability in human assessment—recognized as a confounding factor—will be meticulously addressed to uphold the integrity of experiment outcomes.

ambiguous_choice.defensible_rationale: Academic papers often have diverse temporal patterns and research topics. The stratified random split ensures a balanced representation, making the trained model robust across these variations — suitable when the hypothesis claims improved relevance irrespective of temporal trends.
ambiguous_choice.legitimate_challenge: If temporal autocorrelation is significant—older articles influencing newer ones—a chronological split might be necessary to prevent training data from leaking future patterns, suggesting stratification undermines model validity.
ambiguous_choice.empirical_condition: Measure autocorrelation of evaluation scores at specified publication year lags; if above a threshold of 0.3 for adjacent years, chronological split is required to avert data leakage into training.
